_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
condition: Congenital Heart Disease requiring Fontan Procedure
procedure_name: Surgical Correction of Category - III Congenital Heart Disease (Fontan Procedure)
clinical_indications: Third stage palliation for univentricular heart, forming a total cavo-pulmonary anastomosis or full Fontan circulation to completely separate systemic arterial and venous blood flow.
Total cavopulmonary connection (preferably extracardiac) for children between 4 and 7 years of age, weighing 15-20 kg.
Lateral tunnel Fontan for children aged 3–4 years, if required earlier due to increasing cyanosis or pulmonary arteriovenous malformations.
Fenestration of Fontan circuit is indicated in high-risk cases.

Additional procedures such as repair of AV valve regurgitation, atrial septectomy, branch pulmonary artery stenosis repair (plasty), and TAPVC repair may have to be done at the time of total cavopulmonary connection.

clinical_thresholds.note: If Fontan required earlier due to increasing cyanosis or pulmonary arteriovenous malformations

claim.label: Post procedure stills of ECHO with report

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure?
Was the Echo/ Doppler report suggestive of single ventricle morphology or hypoplastic heart syndrome?

cpd_claim.q: Are the Indoor case papers submitted?
Are the detailed Procedure / Operative notes submitted?
Was post procedure echo report submitted?
Does the Post procedure still of ECHO show rerouting of SVC circulation into right pulmonary artery?
Does the Post procedure still of ECHO show total cavo-pulmonary circulation?
Is there a Detailed Discharge Summary mentioning date of follow-up submitted?

common_queries: Echo/Doppler report not suggestive of single ventricle morphology or hypoplastic heart syndrome.
Clinical notes, detailed history, signs & symptoms, or indication for procedure is missing or inadequate.
Indoor case papers not submitted at claim stage.
Detailed Procedure / Operative notes not submitted at claim stage.
Post-procedure echo report not submitted at claim stage.
Post-procedure still of ECHO does not show rerouting of SVC circulation into the right pulmonary artery.
Post-procedure still of ECHO does not show total cavo-pulmonary circulation.
Detailed Discharge Summary not submitted or does not mention the date of follow-up.

clinical_key_pointers: Univentricular hearts are defined as the presence of one ventricle instead of two, or the second ventricle is rudimentary without an inlet portion.
The main ventricle may be of left (more common) or right ventricular morphology or, in rare cases, indeterminate.
Classical examples include double-inlet left ventricle, tricuspid atresia, and hypoplastic left heart syndrome.

The term also includes congenital heart defects where two-ventricle (or biventricular) repair is not possible, such as unbalanced AVSD, double-outlet RV with a non-routable VSD, straddling of an AV valve, or very large/multiple VSDs not amenable to closure.
These defects are often grouped as “functional univentricular” heart, managed on the lines of single ventricle.
Clinical presentation depends on the ratio of pulmonary-to-systemic blood flow.
Infants with unrestricted pulmonary blood flow present with predominant heart failure and mild cyanosis.
Those with obstruction to pulmonary blood flow have dominant cyanosis and no heart failure.
Rarely, a balanced circulation could be present.
Surgery for univentricular heart is a palliative procedure; life expectancy is less than normal.
Treating physicians must inform and discuss details with the parent/guardian before surgery.
surgical_options: Norwood Procedure (Stage I palliation): Aims to 1) allow unobstructed blood flow from the right ventricle to the systemic circulation, 2) establish connection between systemic and restricted pulmonary circulation, 3) provide unobstructed flow from pulmonary veins through atrial septum to right atrium.

Glenn Procedure (Stage II): Transition from high pressure arterial supply to venous pulmonary blood flow, creating anastomosis between superior vena cava and pulmonary artery. SVC is separated from right atrium and connected to pulmonary artery. BT shunt, if present, is taken down. Pulmonary circulation perfused with fully desaturated blood from systemic venous flow.

Fontan Procedure (Stage III): Total cavo-pulmonary anastomosis or full Fontan circulation. Systemic arterial and venous blood flow completely separated. Inferior vena cava connected to pulmonary artery bypassing the heart. Systemic circulation perfused exclusively with fully saturated blood.
